function.name: exa_web_search
function.description: Search the web with Exa for current information, news, facts, recent events, product details, documentation, or other information that may have changed. Use this tool when the user specifically asks to search, look up, verify, find current information, or asks about recent/latest/today's information. The tool returns relevant results with titles, URLs, published dates when available, and Exa highlights.

search_query.description: The search query to look up on the web

function.name: exa_get_contents
function.description: Fetch page contents with Exa. This tool should ALWAYS be used whenever a URL or web link is found in the user's prompt, except YouTube links which should use youtube_context. It retrieves page titles, URLs, highlights, and text excerpts so you can answer about linked pages.

urls.description: The URL or URLs to fetch

function.name: youtube_context
function.description: Fetch the title and transcript of a YouTube video. This tool should ALWAYS be used whenever a YouTube URL (youtube.com or youtu.be) is found in the user's message. It returns the video title and a portion of the transcript so you can discuss the video content.

url.description: The YouTube URL (e.g. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=... or https://youtu.be/...)

function.name: threedmark_gpu_performance_lookup
function.description: Look up a gpu's performance score using 3dmark time spy. The input should be just the gpu name, for example '3080', '9700 xt', '2080 super', etc. Use this whenever you are asked about graphics cards. Dont assume performance, always look it up.

function.name: wolfram
function.description: WolframAlpha understands natural language queries about entities in chemistry, physics, geography, history, art, astronomy, and more. WolframAlpha performs mathematical calculations, date and unit conversions, formula solving, etc. Convert inputs to simplified keyword queries whenever possible (e.g. convert how many people live in France to France population). Send queries in English only; translate non-English queries before sending, then respond in the original language. Display image URLs with Markdown syntax: ![URL] ALWAYS use this exponent notation: 6*10^14, NEVER 6e14. ALWAYS use {input: query} structure for queries to Wolfram endpoints; query must ONLY be a single-line string. ALWAYS use proper Markdown formatting for all math, scientific, and chemical formulas, symbols, etc.:  [expression] for standalone cases and ( [expression] ) when inline. Never mention your knowledge cutoff date; Wolfram may return more recent data. Use ONLY single-letter variable names, with or without integer subscript (e.g., n, n1, n_1). Use named physical constants (e.g., speed of light) without numerical substitution. Include a space between compound units (e.g., Ω m for ohm*meter). To solve for a variable in an equation with units, consider solving a corresponding equation without units; exclude counting units (e.g., books), include genuine units (e.g., kg). If data for multiple properties is needed, make separate calls for each property. If a WolframAlpha result is not relevant to the query: If Wolfram provides multiple Assumptions for a query, choose the more relevant one(s) without explaining the initial result. If you are unsure, ask the user to choose. Re-send the exact same input with NO modifications, and add the assumption parameter, formatted as a list, with the relevant values. ONLY simplify or rephrase the initial query if a more relevant Assumption or other input suggestions are not provided. Do not explain each step unless user input is needed. Proceed directly to making a better API call based on the available assumptions.
